FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data release 2.0.1; 21-site longitudinal study of children aged 9-10 at baseline; N=4042 with follow-up DRD data.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ML predictive modeling analysis using elastic net, random forest, gradient boosting, and support vector regression to predict delayed reward discounting (DRD) at 1-year follow-up from baseline ABCD questionnaire/task and MRI data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Experimental and Clinical Psychopharmacology; no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is defined as 'the weekly average hours a child typically spends on a computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device' (p.17) — a multi-device aggregated composite that includes TV shows/movies, videos, gaming, texting, social networking, and video chat on any device. Table 3 shows 'Weekend Screen Time (parent report)' (EN B=-1.66), 'Weekday Screen Time (youth report)' (EN B=-1.14), and 'Weekend Screen Time (parent report)' (EN B=-0.98) as predictors of DRD, but these are all the same multi-device aggregate with no per-device or phone/SM-specific breakout anywhere in the paper. Step 2 fails: composite bundles TV, gaming, internet browsing, and non-phone devices without separate breakout.

notes: Screen time composite explicitly defined as hours on 'a computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device' — multi-device aggregated per §2.4 binding clarification 5. No separate cellphone-only or social-media-only coefficient anywhere in the paper including supplementary tables.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses cross-sectional baseline data from U.S. ABCD Study (2016-2018), 21 sites, N=11,875 children aged 9-10, released in 2019; explicitly identified as the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multilevel general linear models and logistic regression examining associations between parental psychological problems and children's screen time behaviors including social media use.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Acta Paediatrica; no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Social media (minutes/day) is analyzed separately from TV, videos, and games in Table 3 with its own coefficient for parental externalising (coeff=2.17, 95% CI -2.25, 6.59), internalising (coeff=0.01, 95% CI -3.80, 3.77), and total problems. The social media variable (visiting social networking sites such as Twitter or Instagram, texting, video chat) is a named SM/phone-specific measure. The other side is parental psychopathology (externalising, internalising, total problems from Adult Self-Report) — which qualifies as individual-trait (parental psychopathology/mental health). FT-IC2 = MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel general linear models report regression coefficients with 95% CIs for social media minutes/day as outcome (Table 3); logistic regression reports ORs with 95% CIs for mature-rated media outcomes (Table 4); quantitative estimates throughout.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported for SM-as-DV analyses: e.g., parental internalising problems → social media minutes/day: coeff=0.01 (95% CI -3.80, 3.77); parental externalising → social media: coeff=2.17 (95% CI -2.25, 6.59). Inferential analysis with numeric results present.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (baseline data only), so the longitudinal condition for FT-EC7 is not met. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: SM (social media minutes/day) is the DV; parental psychopathology is the IV. Cross-sectional design. Social media variable includes visiting social networking sites (Twitter, Instagram), texting, and video chat per YSTS questionnaire. Paper qualifies via social media minutes/day as a separately reported SM-specific variable with its own coefficient.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data release 3.0 from 21 U.S. recruitment sites; N=6749 adolescents aged 12-13 at COVID RRR Survey 1 (May 2020); explicitly identified as the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear regression to estimate associations between ACE score and screen time/physical activity in May 2020, adjusting for sociodemographic and COVID-19 variables.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics; no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate with its own coefficient exists anywhere in the paper. The screen time outcome is a summed total of 7 modalities: 'multiple-player gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing the internet, and watching or streaming movies, videos, or television shows' (p.1295). All analyses use this aggregated total; no separate coefficient for texting, social media, or video chatting is reported. Step 2: The composite fails — it includes gaming (platform unspecified), internet browsing, and TV/streaming alongside texting/social media/video chat, all without breakout. Per §2.4 binding clarification 5, multi-activity composites bundling TV, gaming, and non-phone items without separate breakout fail FT-IC2.

notes: Total screen time composite sums 7 modalities including single/multiplayer gaming (platform unspecified), internet browsing, and TV streaming alongside SM/texting/video chat — no per-modality breakout in results. ACEs (individual-trait) is the IV but the screen time DV fails the composite-content check.
